description:
You are an integration developer who has just received a small,
    pre-recorded benchmark file that contains the response-time
    measurements (in milliseconds) of several REST API calls.  
    Management wants a quick performance summary so they can decide
    whether further optimisation is needed.

    1. The raw data file is already present at  
       /home/user/api_test/data/response_samples.txt

    2. The file is a UTF-8 text file that **always** has:
       • a single header line  
       • one comma-separated value (CSV) record per subsequent line
       
       Exact, verbatim contents of the file you will find on disk:
       ```
       request_id,time_ms
       1,120
       2,110
       3,130
       4,115
       5,140
       6,125
       ```

    3. Your job is to compute four simple statistics from the file:
       • total_requests  (integer)  
       • average_time_ms (float rounded to exactly two decimal places)  
       • fastest_request_ms (integer minimum)  
       • slowest_request_ms (integer maximum)

    4. Write the results to a brand-new file called
       /home/user/api_test/performance_summary.log

       The summary file **must** follow this exact four-line, CSV-like
       format so that automated tests can validate it immediately:

       Line-1: total_requests,<value>
       Line-2: average_time_ms,<value>
       Line-3: fastest_request_ms,<value>
       Line-4: slowest_request_ms,<value>

       Example (using the numbers from the sample file above):
       ```
       total_requests,6
       average_time_ms,123.33
       fastest_request_ms,110
       slowest_request_ms,140
       ```

       • No extra spaces before or after commas.  
       • A single trailing newline at the end of the file.  
       • Do **NOT** include the word “example”; the file you create
         should contain only the four lines shown.

    5. Keep everything in the user’s home directory hierarchy; do not
       require sudo or root privileges.

    6. Use no more than two or three shell commands in total to do all
       the work (e.g., a short Unix pipeline or a tiny shell script is
       fine).  Feel free to leverage standard GNU utilities available in
       most minimal Linux environments.  No external packages should be
       required.

    When you are finished, /home/user/api_test/performance_summary.log
    should exist exactly as specified, ready for automated verification.
truth:
Initial files and directories that exist before the task starts:

    1. Directory: /home/user/api_test
    2. Directory: /home/user/api_test/data
    3. File: /home/user/api_test/data/response_samples.txt  
       Contents (7 lines, terminated by a single newline):
       request_id,time_ms
       1,120
       2,110
       3,130
       4,115
       5,140
       6,125

    Expected artefact created by the agent:

    • File: /home/user/api_test/performance_summary.log  
      Exact contents (including a trailing newline):

      total_requests,6
      average_time_ms,123.33
      fastest_request_ms,110
      slowest_request_ms,140

      The automated test will:
      1. Verify the file exists exactly at the path above.
      2. Read the four lines and compare them byte-for-byte (including
         the single newline at the end) with the string listed above.  
      3. Fail the task if there are additional blank lines, spaces,
         different capitalisation, or numeric formatting that does not
         match precisely.
